channel.title: Comments for page "Figure and Ground"
channel.description: Posts in the discussion thread "Figure and Ground"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think I've accepted that I'm never going to write it, but my preferred headcanon ending involves Navarro's magic fire, Green's cigarette lighter, and an alternate version of SCP-2162 constructed of pure oxygen.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Psul's intent was to leave this ambiguous.

I think that certainly Griffiths thought they might go off the reservation, if given leave under these circumstances and benched them anyway, whether that is cynical CYA, tacit approval, or a setup is ambiguous. I think that these two guys probably thought about trying to save her, and maybe they did try, and maybe they even succeeded. If something like that did officially did not happen, there would never be a report about it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Does anyone know if the author ever followed up on the ending?

Otherwise my headcanon is that the agents save the kids. Because I want a good ending somewhere on this site.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like this!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Pleased you liked it, Jacob, especially in the context of using Navarro. When I wrote this, I wanted to use an established character for the investigation, and I had read tales with either Green or Navarro tracking down anartists - it wasn't until both their creators gave me permission that I realised they might be a good mismatched team.

I felt the ending was both suitably realistic and dark for someone breaking a deal with MC&D.

Score one for "Team Chinatown".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+1

I finally got around to reading this. This really compliments the content of the article its based on, and its neat to see the investigation unfold. In my opinion this is a nice use of Navarro as a character. Additionally, I felt the ending was both suitably realistic and dark for someone breaking a deal with MC&D. Nice job.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+1

I liked it as I read it; it's a good tale. The beginning and ending "bookends" really tied it together for me; I liked them a lot. There are parts of this that I think would really enhance your SCP if included as supplemental information, even on the SCP page itself. The journal diary entries for 30 June 2013 and 2 July 2013, for instance, would be a great "teaser" to get people to read your tale, while still keeping most of the mystery in the tale itself, and while still being relevant in a skip summation page.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: All I can say is that I had no context for the first paragraph, and it faded from memory as I worked through what is by all means just a series of addenda. The ending tied things together, but I stand by my statement.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Fair enough. Thanks for reading on after the SCP, thanks very much for sharing your thoughts, and I'm glad that you liked the procedural at least.

I didn't really feel anything for Baker at the end other than a vague "huh, that's unfortunate."

That is a shame. Part of the formal challenge I set myself here was to make Robert Baker a character (and ideally a somewhat sympathetic, somewhat nuanced character) without him ever appearing in the story - to be blatantly expository, the idea was to have his story as the ground and the procedural be the figure. Clearly character is something I can work on for my next submission.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This does expand the story and helps to explain some of the why, but the why isn't particularly interesting.

There's nothing particularly distinctive about this narrative. "Eager beaver young person gets involved in anomalous stuff, gets in over their head1" isn't really a new story on the wiki. I didn't really feel anything for Baker at the end other than a vague "huh, that's unfortunate." Basically, this feels like a largely uninteresting expansion to an already-uninteresting story.

I will say that I liked the actual procedural stuff, though. It's nice to see the Foundation find stuff out with handwaving it ("something something hacking communications something something surveillance"). Still, I gotta go with a downvote.

Footnotes
1. Double points if the person is an anartist

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
For me, those two paragraphs serve three purposes.

  • Most importantly, now that the story is split, the first one provides a little bit of narrative momentum to the re-start of the procedural. By showing that there is human life at risk, the reader is reassured that this isn't merely going to be forensic analysis of gaseous constructs. It's a trick I've stolen from TV, which is appropriate, I think.
  • Secondly, I agree with sirpudding that (having decided to put them in), I wanted to use them to emphasise that this is the moment at which the story-telling is at its highest point of tension. If this is noir, then the story ends at that point, with no happy ending and no catharsis. If it is another genre, then perhaps June's question will be answered - someone will come.

(As an aside on this point, I will take sirpudding's question and raise it: "What did Assistant-Director Griffiths think would happen when he put them on administrative leave?")

  • Finally, apart from my somewhat heavy-handed literalisation of the title, I also want to reinforce the theme here. The reader's understanding of the story is based on how they interpret the context of the characters' actions, and how they interpret the characters themselves. Is A-D Griffiths simply a heavy-handed bureaucrat, or is he something else? (One interpretation might be that he's actually working with MC&D). Is this the end of the story, or just the end of Act 2? The final question - would no-one come? - is rhetorical in more than just the sense that June's asking it to herself. It is me as author asking it of the audience - what image do you see in this picture?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks - I knew some of the numbering would get messed up when I switched the order of the addenda. Error fixed, and a formal protest summarily dismissed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
rather than recovery of PoI-2162-1 and PoI-2162-3.

Should be "rather than recovery of PoI-2162-1 and PoI-2162-2." -3 was the late Cavendish.

Edit: Also, I still think that last bit would have more punch if it included a mention of a formal protest by one (Navarro?) or both agents.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The whole "Is this the end of Chinatown or the beginning of the third act of Lethal Weapon?" thing is less obvious without it. What are these two hard-boiled occult investigators going to do with their administrative leave?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Man, I'm really bad at this sometimes.

That said, other than tying up "what happened to the PoIs", I think those two paragraphs could be lost while retaining the bulk of the story.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Mr and Mrs Baker advised that June had "gone to see her brother in Los Angeles", but were vague as to the details or timing of the visit.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The opening and ending I can't make heads or tails of. If you stripped those off and put the rest in with the article itself, I think you'd have a very excellent, complete story of a skip.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Clearly it is made up of supplementary materials, and I don't think it makes sense without having read the SCP first, which suggests that it is a Supplement.

However its purpose is to tell a story, and I also think it needs the bookend as a bit of an additional hook (and restatement of the theme), so Tale also seems appropriate.

In the end, it falls somewhere in between, so I am happy to be guided by consensus on how to label it. Could it have both labels, or is that taboo?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The noir-ish narrative bits with the sister at the beginning and end?

On that note, I am not sure if it should breadcrumb back to the SCP. Should it?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Interesting story for certain. However, I must ask: Why is this classified as a Tale instead of a supplement?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This definitely came out as a neat noir procedural. I really like this quite a bit! I think if someone wanted to make a Foundation film, this would make a perfect one.

Edit: I set the parent for you, by the way.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is an expansion of the story started in SCP-2162. It's about looking at the same thing in different ways, and what that can reveal.

Attributions: As I said in the skip comments, very many thanks to:

  • Randomini for letting me use Agent Green. My headcanon is that this is set a little before The Cool War.
  • TwistedGears for letting me use Agent Navarro. I hadn't read all of the Navarro-starring work before writing this, so it probably skews closest to the Navarro of Learning the Alphabet.

When I was thinking about an agent for this investigation, I asked for permission from both TG and Randomini, as I knew both Green and Navarro had a connection to anart. I never imagined that I would get permission from both of them, and the more I thought about it, the more I liked the idea of the two characters working together.

"Michael Cavendish" is with permission from and great thanks to Rioghail, whose SCP-2071 includes the original character (from the early 1800s) and provided mine with an alias.

Acknowledgements: Thanks also to everyone who reviewed this on the forums and chat. Decibelles, WrongJohnSilver, Dr Hood, Dr Solo and sirpudding.
